title: Rune Translator
instructions: Ancient runes are encoded text. Slice and index strings to translate them. Every character has a position.
whyThisMatters: Strings are sequences of characters. Each character has an index starting at 0 (zero-based indexing). 'hello'[0] = 'h', 'hello'[1] = 'e'.
template:
# ========================================
# RUNE TRANSLATOR
# Level 4 — The Awakening
# Concept: String Operations & Indexing
# ========================================
# Ancient runes are encoded text. Slice and index strings to translate them. Every character has a position.
#
# Theory: Strings are sequences of characters. Each character has an index starting at 0 (zero-based indexing). 'hello'[0] = 'h', 'hello'[1] = 'e'.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# Take the string 'GALACTIC' and print the first 4 characters using slicing: text[0:4]

# Print the last 4 characters using negative indexing: text[-4:]

# Combine slices to spell 'ACTIC' from 'GALACTIC' and print it
errorTips:
Indexing starts at 0: 'hello'[0] = 'h', 'hello'[1] = 'e'
Slicing [start:end] — end is exclusive: 'hello'[0:2] = 'he'
Negative index counts from end: 'hello'[-1] = 'o', 'hello'[-4:] = 'ello'
Slicing [start:] from start to end; [:end] from beginning to end

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Python'[0] return?
What does 'Python'[1:4] return?
Why do computers start counting from 0 instead of 1?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Because index 0 means 'offset 0' — skip 0 positions to get the first character. Index 1 means skip 1 position. It makes the math cleaner: slice length = end - start. It also maps directly to how memory offsets work.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Star Wars'[-4:] return?

promptChallenge.rubric: Specificity (2pts): asks specific question + analogy | Clarity (1pt): clear structure | Context (2pts): describes confusion

# Calculate fuel = 500 light years × 2.5 units per light year. Store in a variable and print.

# 12 crew, each needs 3 rations/day for 7 days. Use integer division // for whole rations.

# Find the remainder of 17 ÷ 5 using % (modulo). Print the remainder.
errorTips:
Use variables: distance = 500, rate = 2.5, then total = distance * rate
// is integer division (floor): 17 // 5 = 3 (drops decimals)
% is modulo (remainder): 17 % 5 = 2
PEMDAS: use parentheses () to control the order of operations

quizQuestions.question: What does 17 // 5 return?
What does 17 % 5 return?
What is the result of (10 + 5) * 2 - 4?
Why would a programmer use integer division // instead of regular division /?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: When you need whole numbers only. You can't have 3.7 crew members — // gives 3 (floor), / gives 3.4 (float). Integer division is essential for counting discrete items like people, items, or ships.

quizQuestions.question: Why is indentation important in Python?

continue skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next
if i % 3 == 0: continue — skips multiples of 3
break and continue work with both for and while loops

What does this print?

    if i == 3: break
    print(i)
What is the difference between break and continue?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: break exits the loop entirely — no more iterations happen. continue skips only the rest of the current iteration and immediately starts the next iteration.

quizQuestions.question:
What does this print?
for i in range(1, 6):
    if i == 3: continue
    print(i)

instructions: Set armor defense level with a default. Default parameters make functions more flexible.

template:
# ========================================

#

# ========================================

# Define: def configure_shield(strength=100): that prints the strength

errorTips:
Default: def func(x=10): — x is 10 if no argument given
Call with: func() uses default, func(20) uses 20
Variables inside function are local — separate from outside
To modify global: global variable_name inside the function

quizQuestions.q:
What prints?

greet()
What is a local variable?

template:

#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.

# Define a function: def greet(): that prints 'Welcome aboard!'

Welcome aboard!
Welcome aboard!
Welcome aboard!
300

def function_name(): — colon starts the body

return value: return expression

What does this print?

quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: To organize code into reusable, named blocks. Define once, use many times. Reduces repetition and makes code easier to read and debug.

title: Engine Power Calculator
instructions: Calculate engine thrust based on fuel flow rate. Parameters: inputs the function receives. Arguments: values you pass when calling.
whyThisMatters: Parameters are the named inputs in the function definition. Arguments are the actual values passed at call time. Position matters: the first argument goes to the first parameter.
template:
# ========================================
# ENGINE POWER CALCULATOR
# Level 22 — Functions
# Concept: Parameters & arguments
# ========================================
# Calculate engine thrust based on fuel flow rate. Parameters: inputs the function receives. Arguments: values you pass when calling.
#
# Theory: Parameters are the named inputs in the function definition. Arguments are the actual values passed at call time. Position matters: the first argument goes to the first parameter.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# Define: def calculate_thrust(fuel_flow, efficiency): that returns fuel_flow * efficiency * 10

# Call it with arguments: calculate_thrust(25, 0.8)

# Print the result
errorTips:
def func(param1, param2): — parameters listed in definition
func(arg1, arg2) — arguments passed at call time
Return: return fuel_flow * efficiency * 10
Parameters and arguments match by position

quizQuestions.q: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: A parameter is the variable name in the function definition. An argument is the actual value you pass when calling the function. Parameters are placeholders; arguments are real values.

quizQuestions.q:
How many arguments does this pass?
result = calculate(10, 20, 30)

What is printed?
def add(a, b):
    return a + b
print(add(3, 4))

Access: dict['key'] — error if missing

# ========================================

'key' in dict — True if key exists, False otherwise

for x in items: count[x] = count.get(x,0)+1

# ========================================
# QUEST SCROLL READER
# Level 71 — File I/O & OS
# Concept: Reading files — open, read, close
# ========================================
# Read quest entries from a scroll file. Use open(), read(), and close(). Handle missing files.
#
# Theory: Reading files in Python: f = open('file.txt') / content = f.read() / f.close(). Or use with open('file.txt') as f: content = f.read() - the with block auto-closes the file. Modes: 'r' read, 'w' write (truncates), 'a' append.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# with open('mission.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()
print(content)

# with open('mission.txt', 'r') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()
for line in lines: print(line.strip())

# # Safe reading with error handling
try:
    with open('mission.txt', 'r') as f:
        print(f.read())
except FileNotFoundError:
    print('Log not found')
errorTips:
open() needs a mode: 'r' for reading, 'w' for writing
Always use with block for auto-close: with open(...) as f:
read() returns entire content as one string
readlines() returns a list of lines (with newline chars)

quizQuestions.q: Why is it better to use 'with open(...) as f:' instead of just 'f = open(...)'?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: The with block auto-closes the file when exiting, even if an exception occurs. With plain open(), you must remember to call f.close() - if you forget or an error occurs before close(), the file stays open and data may not be flushed.

quizQuestions.q: What does open('file.txt', 'r') return if the file does not exist?

options: B) read() returns a list of lines, readlines() returns a string

Mode 'w' overwrites the file if it exists - data is lost!

options: A) An automatic newline is added

title: Mission Log Reader
instructions: Read mission logs from a text file. Use open(), read(), and close(). Handle missing files gracefully.
whyThisMatters: Reading files in Python: f = open('file.txt') / content = f.read() / f.close(). Or use with open('file.txt') as f: content = f.read() - the with block auto-closes the file. Modes: 'r' read, 'w' write (truncates), 'a' append.
template:
# ========================================
# MISSION LOG READER
# Level 71 — File I/O & OS
# Concept: Reading files — open, read, close
# ========================================
# Read mission logs from a text file. Use open(), read(), and close(). Handle missing files gracefully.
#
# Theory: Reading files in Python: f = open('file.txt') / content = f.read() / f.close(). Or use with open('file.txt') as f: content = f.read() - the with block auto-closes the file. Modes: 'r' read, 'w' write (truncates), 'a' append.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# with open('mission.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()
print(content)

# with open('mission.txt', 'r') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()
for line in lines: print(line.strip())

# # Safe reading with error handling
try:
    with open('mission.txt', 'r') as f:
        print(f.read())
except FileNotFoundError:
    print('Log not found')
errorTips:
open() needs a mode: 'r' for reading, 'w' for writing
Always use with block for auto-close: with open(...) as f:
read() returns entire content as one string
readlines() returns a list of lines (with newline chars)

quizQuestions.q: Why is it better to use 'with open(...) as f:' instead of just 'f = open(...)'?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: The with block auto-closes the file when exiting, even if an exception occurs. With plain open(), you must remember to call f.close() - if you forget or an error occurs before close(), the file stays open and data may not be flushed.

quizQuestions.q: What does open('file.txt', 'r') return if the file does not exist?
What is the difference between f.read() and f.readlines()?
options: B) read() returns a list of lines, readlines() returns a string
C) read() returns the entire content as one string, readlines() returns a list of lines

title: Mission Logger
instructions: Write mission reports to a file. Use open() with 'w' mode to create or overwrite files.
whyThisMatters: Writing files: open('file.txt', 'w') opens for writing (creates or truncates). open('file.txt', 'a') opens for appending (creates or adds to end). f.write('text') writes text without newline - add '\n' explicitly. f.writelines(list) writes all lines.
template:
# ========================================
# MISSION LOGGER
# Level 72 — File I/O & OS
# Concept: Writing files — creating and overwriting
# ========================================
# Write mission reports to a file. Use open() with 'w' mode to create or overwrite files.
#
# Theory: Writing files: open('file.txt', 'w') opens for writing (creates or truncates). open('file.txt', 'a') opens for appending (creates or adds to end). f.write('text') writes text without newline - add '\n' explicitly. f.writelines(list) writes all lines.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# with open('report.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Mission Complete\n')
    f.write('Rating: 5 stars\n')
print('Report saved')

# lines = ['Alpha\n', 'Beta\n', 'Gamma\n']
with open('sectors.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.writelines(lines)

# # Append mode - adds to existing file
with open('log.txt', 'a') as f:
    f.write('New entry\n')
errorTips:
Mode 'w' overwrites the file if it exists - data is lost!
Mode 'a' appends to the end - keeps existing data
write() does NOT add newline - use '\n' explicitly
writelines() expects a list - writes each element without adding newlines

quizQuestions.q: You open a file with open('data.txt', 'w'), write 'Hello', then open it again with 'w' and write 'World'. What does the file contain?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Only 'World' - the second open with 'w' truncates (deletes) the file contents before writing. Each 'w' mode opens starts fresh. If you want to add content, use 'a' (append) mode instead.

quizQuestions.q: What happens if you use write() without adding '\n' at the end?
options: A) An automatic newline is added
B) The next write() continues on the same line

# ========================================
# SPELL ERROR HANDLER
# Level 46 — Errors & Debugging
# Concept: try and except blocks
# ========================================
# Catch spell casting errors so the program continues. Errors are normal — handle them.
#
# Theory: try: risky code
except ErrorType: handle it
Errors stop execution unless caught. Catching specific error types is better than bare except.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# try: result = 10 / 0
except ZeroDivisionError: print('Cannot divide by zero')

# try: nums = [1,2,3]; print(nums[5])
except IndexError: print('Index out of range')

# A try/except that catches any error: try: int('hello')
except Exception as e: print('Error:', type(e).__name__)
targetOutput:
Cannot divide by zero
Index out of range
Error: ValueError
errorTips:
try: risky code, except: handle error
except ZeroDivisionError: catches division by zero
except IndexError: catches out-of-range list access
Bare except: catches ALL errors (not recommended)

quizQuestions.q: What happens if an error occurs inside a try block with no matching except?
What is the difference between except ZeroDivisionError and a bare except:
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: except ZeroDivisionError catches only that specific error type. Bare except: catches all exceptions, which can hide bugs. Always catch specific errors when you know what to expect.

else: runs if NO exception was raised

# ========================================

# Handle both IndexError and KeyError: try: x = [1,2,3]; x[10]
except IndexError: print('Bad index')
except KeyError: print('Bad key')

# Add: try: d = {'a':1}; d['z']
except IndexError: print('idx')
except KeyError: print('key missing')

# Use else: try: x = 5
except: pass
else: print('Success — no error')

pass: do nothing in the except block
except Exception as e: catches everything

template:
# ========================================
# SENSOR ERROR HANDLER
# Level 46 — Errors & Debugging
# Concept: try and except blocks
# ========================================
# Handle sensor reading errors gracefully. try/except catches errors so the program doesn't crash.
#
# Theory: try: risky code
except ErrorType: handle it
Errors stop execution unless caught. Catching specific error types is better than bare except.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# try: result = 10 / 0
except ZeroDivisionError: print('Cannot divide by zero')

# try: nums = [1,2,3]; print(nums[5])
except IndexError: print('Index out of range')

# A try/except that catches any error: try: int('hello')
except Exception as e: print('Error:', type(e).__name__)
targetOutput:
Cannot divide by zero
Index out of range
Error: ValueError
errorTips:
try: risky code, except: handle error
except ZeroDivisionError: catches division by zero
except IndexError: catches out-of-range list access
Bare except: catches ALL errors (not recommended)

quizQuestions.q: What happens if an error occurs inside a try block with no matching except?
What is the difference between except ZeroDivisionError and a bare except:
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: except ZeroDivisionError catches only that specific error type. Bare except: catches all exceptions, which can hide bugs. Always catch specific errors when you know what to expect.

# ========================================
# MULTI-SENSOR HANDLER
# Level 47 — Errors & Debugging
# Concept: Multiple except and else
# ========================================
# Handle multiple error types separately. Different errors need different responses.
#
# Theory: try:
  code
except ValueError: handle value errors
except KeyError: handle missing keys
except Exception as e: catch all others
else: runs if NO exception was raised
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# Handle both IndexError and KeyError: try: x = [1,2,3]; x[10]
except IndexError: print('Bad index')
except KeyError: print('Bad key')

# Add: try: d = {'a':1}; d['z']
except IndexError: print('idx')
except KeyError: print('key missing')

# Use else: try: x = 5
except: pass
else: print('Success — no error')
errorTips:
except catches in order — first matching one runs
else: runs only if no exception was raised
pass: do nothing in the except block
except Exception as e: catches everything

quizQuestions.q: What is the purpose of the else clause in a try/except block?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: The else clause runs ONLY if the try block completed without raising any exception. It's for code that should run on success but not on failure.

quizQuestions.q: In what order does Python check except clauses?
options: B) In the order they appear — first matching one handles the error

# ========================================

# ========================================
# Raise an error when something is wrong. raise forces an exception — useful for invalid input.

quizQuestions.q: What does raise do without an argument inside an except block?

# ========================================
# Make Ship objects print nicely. __str__ controls what print() shows; __repr__ for debugging.

#

# Press Run to test your solution.

# class Ship:
    def __init__(self, name, fuel):
        self.name = name
        self.fuel = fuel
    def __str__(self):

# s = Ship('Titanic', 60); print(s)

__str__ — user-friendly output, used by print()
__repr__ — developer-friendly, used in REPL and debugging

title: Protected Ship Data
instructions: Protect ship data from outside changes. Single underscore = internal hint; double underscore = name mangling.
whyThisMatters: self._attr = 'internal use' — convention: don't touch outside class. self.__attr = 'private' — name mangling: _ClassName__attr (harder to access from outside). Getters and setters control access.
template:
# ========================================
# PROTECTED SHIP DATA
# Level 55 — OOP Basics
# Concept: Encapsulation — private attributes
# ========================================
# Protect ship data from outside changes. Single underscore = internal hint; double underscore = name mangling.
#
# Theory: self._attr = 'internal use' — convention: don't touch outside class. self.__attr = 'private' — name mangling: _ClassName__attr (harder to access from outside). Getters and setters control access.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# class Ship:
    def __init__(self, fuel):
        self.__fuel = fuel  # private — name mangled
    def get_fuel(self):
        return self.__fuel

# s = Ship(100); print(s.get_fuel())

# Try: s.__fuel — this won't work as expected (attribute is mangled to _Ship__fuel). Use the getter instead.
errorTips:
self._attr = convention for 'internal' (single underscore)
self.__attr = name mangling (double underscore)
Access: s._Ship__fuel works but is discouraged
Best: provide get/set methods to control access

quizQuestions.q: What does name mangling do in Python?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Double underscore attributes (_ClassName__attr) are renamed by Python to make them harder to access from outside the class. It's a soft protection — not a security feature, just a convention.

quizQuestions.q: What is the Pythonic way to provide controlled access to a private attribute?

# ========================================
# WARRIOR CHARACTER CLASS
# Level 56 — Inheritance
# Concept: Single inheritance — parent and child classes
# ========================================
# Create a Warrior class that inherits from Character. Inheritance lets you specialize classes.

# Press Run to test your solution.

# class Ship:
    def __init__(self, name, fuel):
        self.name = name
        self.fuel = fuel
    def fly(self):
        return f'{self.name} flies on {self.fuel}% fuel'

# class FighterShip(Ship):
    def __init__(self, name, fuel, weapons):
        super().__init__(name, fuel)  # call parent's __init__
        self.weapons = weapons

# Create: f = FighterShip('Falcon', 80, 'laser'); print(f.fly()); print('Weapons:', f.weapons)
targetOutput:
Falcon flies on 80% fuel
Weapons: laser
errorTips:
class FighterShip(Ship): — inherits from Ship
super().__init__(name, fuel) — calls the parent's __init__
FighterShip has both its own __init__ and Ship.fly()
Child can add new attributes (weapons) and use parent methods (fly)

quizQuestions.q: What does class FighterShip(Ship) mean?

quizQuestions.q: What is super().__init__() used for in a child class?

template:
# ========================================

# Level 57 — Inheritance
# Concept: Method overriding and super()
# ========================================
# Override a parent heal() method in a Healer character with special healing abilities.
#
# Theory: Override: define method with same name in child class. Use super().method(args) to call parent's version first, then add behavior. child.method() calls child's version, not parent's.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# class Ship:
    def describe(self):
        return f'{self.name}: standard ship'

# class CargoShip(Ship):
    def __init__(self, name, fuel, cargo):
        super().__init__(name, fuel)
        self.cargo = cargo
    def describe(self):
        # Override: call parent then add cargo info

        return base + f', carrying {self.cargo}'

# c = CargoShip('Freighter', 60, 'supplies'); print(c.describe())
errorTips:
Same method name in child overrides parent
super().describe() calls parent's version first
Override to extend behavior, not just replace
CargoShip.describe() returns both base info and cargo

quizQuestions.q: What does super().describe() do inside CargoShip.describe()?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: It calls the parent class's describe() method and gets its return value. Then we add to that return value instead of replacing it entirely.

instructions: A hero with both Warrior and Mage abilities through multiple inheritance.
whyThisMatters: class Child(Parent1, Parent2): — inherits from both. MRO (Method Resolution Order): Python checks left parent first, then right. If both have the same method, left parent wins. Diamond problem resolved by MRO.
template:
# ========================================
# MULTI-CLASS HERO
# Level 58 — Inheritance
# Concept: Multiple inheritance and MRO
# ========================================
# A hero with both Warrior and Mage abilities through multiple inheritance.
#
# Theory: class Child(Parent1, Parent2): — inherits from both. MRO (Method Resolution Order): Python checks left parent first, then right. If both have the same method, left parent wins. Diamond problem resolved by MRO.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# class Vehicle:
    def move(self): return 'Moving'
class Communicator:
    def move(self): return 'Broadcasting'
class HybridShip(Vehicle, Communicator):
    pass

# h = HybridShip(); print(h.move())  # MRO: Vehicle comes first

# Use the other method explicitly: Communicator.move(h) or via super: print(HybridShip.__mro__)

class Hybrid(Parent1, Parent2): — multiple parents
MRO: left-to-right — Vehicle.move is used first
HybridShip.__mro__ shows the full resolution order
Both parents' attributes are available

quizQuestions.q: In class HybridShip(Vehicle, Communicator), which parent's move() is called?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Vehicle's move(), because Vehicle is listed first in the parentheses. MRO is left-to-right — Python checks Vehicle first, finds move(), and uses it.

quizQuestions.q: What is the 'diamond problem' in multiple inheritance?
options: A) When a class has no parent
B) When two parents inherit from the same grandparent and a child inherits from both — creating ambiguity about which grandparent method to use
C) When a class name is too long
D) When methods have the same name

quizQuestions.q: What does ClassName.__mro__ return?
options: A) All methods in the class

title: Hero Classification Check
instructions: Verify character types using isinstance() and issubclass(). Check inheritance relationships.
whyThisMatters: isinstance(obj, Class) — True if obj is an instance of Class or its subclasses. issubclass(Child, Parent) — True if Child inherits from Parent. Use these instead of type() == Class.
template:
# ========================================
# HERO CLASSIFICATION CHECK
# Level 59 — Inheritance
# Concept: isinstance and issubclass
# ========================================
# Verify character types using isinstance() and issubclass(). Check inheritance relationships.
#
# Theory: isinstance(obj, Class) — True if obj is an instance of Class or its subclasses. issubclass(Child, Parent) — True if Child inherits from Parent. Use these instead of type() == Class.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# class Ship: pass
class FighterShip(Ship): pass
s = Ship(); f = FighterShip()

# Print: isinstance(s, Ship), isinstance(f, Ship), isinstance(f, FighterShip)

# Print: issubclass(FighterShip, Ship), issubclass(Ship, FighterShip)
errorTips:
isinstance(obj, Class) — True if obj IS a Class (or subclass) instance
issubclass(Child, Parent) — True if Child inherits from Parent
isinstance(f, Ship) is True — FighterShip IS a Ship too
type() == Class is too strict — doesn't count subclasses

quizQuestions.q: What does isinstance(FighterShip(), Ship) return and why?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: True, because FighterShip is a subclass of Ship. isinstance() returns True for any instance of a class OR its subclasses. A FighterShip object IS a Ship.

quizQuestions.q: What is the difference between isinstance(obj, Ship) and type(obj) == Ship?
options: B) type() == Ship is False for FighterShip; isinstance is True
C) isinstance only works with integers

quizQuestions.q: What does issubclass(FighterShip, Ship) check?
options: A) If a FighterShip object equals a Ship object
B) If FighterShip is a subclass (inherits from) Ship
C) If a Ship is a FighterShip
D) If both classes are the same

title: Character With Inventory Component
instructions: A Character has an Inventory object rather than inheriting inventory behavior.
whyThisMatters: Inheritance: class B(A) — B IS A A. Composition: class B: def __init__(self): self.engine = Engine() — B HAS AN Engine. Prefer composition: more flexible, less coupling. 'Has-a' vs 'Is-a'.
template:
# ========================================
# CHARACTER WITH INVENTORY COMPONENT
# Level 60 — Inheritance
# Concept: Composition vs inheritance
# ========================================
# A Character has an Inventory object rather than inheriting inventory behavior.
#
# Theory: Inheritance: class B(A) — B IS A A. Composition: class B: def __init__(self): self.engine = Engine() — B HAS AN Engine. Prefer composition: more flexible, less coupling. 'Has-a' vs 'Is-a'.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# class Engine:
    def start(self): return 'Engine started'
class Ship:
    def __init__(self):
        self.engine = Engine()  # composition: Ship HAS an Engine
    def launch(self):
        return self.engine.start()

# s = Ship(); print(s.launch())

# Note the difference: inheritance = FighterShip IS A Ship. Composition = Ship HAS AN Engine.
errorTips:
'Is-a': inheritance — FighterShip is a type of Ship
'Has-a': composition — Ship contains an Engine
Prefer composition when you can — more flexible
A Ship can work with any Engine-like object (duck typing)

quizQuestions.q: When should you prefer composition over inheritance?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Composition (has-a) is preferred when a class needs to USE another class's functionality rather than BEING a type of it. More flexible, less coupled — you can swap the Engine for a BetterEngine without rewriting Ship.

quizQuestions.q: Which describes composition: class Book has a Page, or class Book is a Library?
options: A) Book is a Library — composition
B) Book has a Page — composition (Book HAS a Page)

quizQuestions.q: What is the main disadvantage of deep inheritance hierarchies?
options: B) Tight coupling — children depend heavily on parent implementation details, making changes difficult

title: Ship With Engine Component
instructions: A Ship has an Engine object (composition) vs Ship extends Engine (inheritance).
whyThisMatters: Inheritance: class B(A) — B IS A A. Composition: class B: def __init__(self): self.engine = Engine() — B HAS AN Engine. Prefer composition: more flexible, less coupling. 'Has-a' vs 'Is-a'.
template:
# ========================================
# SHIP WITH ENGINE COMPONENT
# Level 60 — Inheritance
# Concept: Composition vs inheritance
# ========================================
# A Ship has an Engine object (composition) vs Ship extends Engine (inheritance).
#
# Theory: Inheritance: class B(A) — B IS A A. Composition: class B: def __init__(self): self.engine = Engine() — B HAS AN Engine. Prefer composition: more flexible, less coupling. 'Has-a' vs 'Is-a'.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# class Engine:
    def start(self): return 'Engine started'
class Ship:
    def __init__(self):
        self.engine = Engine()  # composition: Ship HAS an Engine
    def launch(self):
        return self.engine.start()

# s = Ship(); print(s.launch())

# Note the difference: inheritance = FighterShip IS A Ship. Composition = Ship HAS AN Engine.
errorTips:
'Is-a': inheritance — FighterShip is a type of Ship
'Has-a': composition — Ship contains an Engine
Prefer composition when you can — more flexible
A Ship can work with any Engine-like object (duck typing)

quizQuestions.q: When should you prefer composition over inheritance?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Composition (has-a) is preferred when a class needs to USE another class's functionality rather than BEING a type of it. More flexible, less coupled — you can swap the Engine for a BetterEngine without rewriting Ship.

quizQuestions.q: Which describes composition: class Book has a Page, or class Book is a Library?
options: A) Book is a Library — composition
B) Book has a Page — composition (Book HAS a Page)

quizQuestions.q: What is the main disadvantage of deep inheritance hierarchies?
options: B) Tight coupling — children depend heavily on parent implementation details, making changes difficult

# ========================================
# BATTLE MATH UTILITY
# Level 63 — Advanced OOP
# Concept: @staticmethod — utility functions in a class
# ========================================
# Use @staticmethod for pure calculation functions that don't need self or cls.
#
# Theory: @staticmethod
def distance(x1, y1, x2, y2): — no self, no cls. It's just a function that happens to live inside the class namespace. Use when the function logically belongs to the class but doesn't need its data.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# import math
class Navigator:
    @staticmethod
    def distance(x1, y1, x2, y2):
        return math.sqrt((x2-x1)**2 + (y2-y1)**2)

# d = Navigator.distance(0, 0, 3, 4); print(d)  # 5.0

# Note: no self, no cls. Call as Navigator.distance() — not on an instance.
errorTips:
@staticmethod — no self, no cls
Call as Navigator.distance() directly, not on an instance
Use for functions that logically belong with the class but don't need its data
Static methods can be called on instances too: nav.distance() works

quizQuestions.q: When should you use @staticmethod instead of a regular function outside the class?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: When the function is conceptually related to the class (like distance() for a Navigator, or validate_email() for a User class) but doesn't need any class or instance data. It groups related utilities under the class namespace.

title: Mana Property Manager
instructions: Use property setters and deleters to manage character stats with validation.
whyThisMatters: @attr.setter — runs when obj.attr = value. @attr.deleter — runs when del obj.attr. Enforce invariants: if value invalid, raise ValueError. Clean up resources on deletion.
template:
# ========================================
# MANA PROPERTY MANAGER
# Level 64 — Advanced OOP
# Concept: Property setters and deleters
# ========================================
# Use property setters and deleters to manage character stats with validation.
#
# Theory: @attr.setter — runs when obj.attr = value. @attr.deleter — runs when del obj.attr. Enforce invariants: if value invalid, raise ValueError. Clean up resources on deletion.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# class Ship:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self._name = name
    @property
    def name(self):
        return self._name
    @name.setter
    def name(self, value):
        if not value: raise ValueError('Name cannot be empty')
        self._name = value
    @name.deleter
    def name(self):
        print(f'Deleting name: {self._name}')
        del self._name

# s = Ship('Voyager'); print(s.name)
s.name = 'Enterprise'; print(s.name)
try: s.name = ''
except ValueError as e: print('Error:', e)

# del s.name — calls the deleter. Try to access s.name after.
targetOutput:
Voyager
Enterprise
Error: Name cannot be empty
Deleting name: Enterprise
errorTips:
@name.setter — runs on assignment: obj.name = value
@name.deleter — runs on del obj.name
Setter: raise ValueError if value is invalid
Deleter: clean up or log before removing attribute

quizQuestions.q: When should you raise an error in a property setter?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: When the assigned value violates an invariant — a rule that must always hold. For example, if a name must be non-empty, age must be non-negative, or a status must be one of a set of valid values.

quizQuestions.q: What does del obj.name do if a deleter is defined?
options: C) Calls the deleter function you defined with @name.deleter

quizQuestions.q: Can a property be read-only (no setter)?
options: A) No — all properties have setters
B) Yes — simply don't define a @name.setter
D) No — properties are always read-write

# class Ship:
    def __init__(self, name, fuel):

        self._fuel = fuel

    def fuel(self): return self._fuel
    @fuel.setter

print(Ship.format_status(s.fuel))

title: Party Sort System
instructions: Sort party members by level using merge sort. Recursive sorting with a merge step.
whyThisMatters: Merge sort: O(n log n) - divide list in half recursively until single elements, then merge sorted halves back together. def merge_sort(arr): if len(arr) <= 1: return arr; mid = len(arr)//2; return merge(merge_sort(arr[:mid]), merge_sort(arr[mid:]))
template:
# ========================================
# PARTY SORT SYSTEM
# Level 68 — Recursion & Sorting
# Concept: Merge sort — divide and conquer
# ========================================
# Sort party members by level using merge sort. Recursive sorting with a merge step.
#
# Theory: Merge sort: O(n log n) - divide list in half recursively until single elements, then merge sorted halves back together. def merge_sort(arr): if len(arr) <= 1: return arr; mid = len(arr)//2; return merge(merge_sort(arr[:mid]), merge_sort(arr[mid:]))
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1: return arr
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    left = merge_sort(arr[:mid])
    right = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    return merge(left, right)

def merge(a, b):
    result = []
    while a and b:
        result.append(a.pop(0) if a[0] <= b[0] else b.pop(0))
    result.extend(a or b)
    return result

# print(merge_sort([38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]))

# Note: merge sort is O(n log n) - consistent regardless of input order.
errorTips:
Merge sort is O(n log n) - consistent performance
Divide: split list in half until single elements
Conquer: sort small lists
Combine: merge two sorted lists - the key step
Base case: len <= 1 is already sorted

quizQuestions.q: Why is merge sort O(n log n) and not O(n squared)?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Because the list is divided log n times (halving until single elements), and at each of the n levels, every element is processed once during the merge step. log n divisions times n work per level = n log n.

quizQuestions.q: What is the base case in merge_sort?
Which is faster: merge sort O(n log n) or bubble sort O(n squared)?
options: B) Merge sort is much faster for large lists

title: Potion Quick Sort
instructions: Sort potion ingredients by potency using quick sort. Pivot-based recursive sorting.
whyThisMatters: Quick sort: O(n log n) average. Pick last element as pivot. Partition: elements less than pivot go left, elements greater than pivot go right. Recurse on left and right partitions.
template:
# ========================================
# POTION QUICK SORT
# Level 69 — Recursion & Sorting
# Concept: Quick sort — partition and conquer
# ========================================
# Sort potion ingredients by potency using quick sort. Pivot-based recursive sorting.
#
# Theory: Quick sort: O(n log n) average. Pick last element as pivot. Partition: elements less than pivot go left, elements greater than pivot go right. Recurse on left and right partitions.
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# def quick_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1: return arr
    pivot = arr[-1]
    less = [x for x in arr[:-1] if x <= pivot]
    greater = [x for x in arr[:-1] if x > pivot]
    return quick_sort(less) + [pivot] + quick_sort(greater)

# print(quick_sort([30, 10, 80, 50, 20, 60, 40]))

# Note: pivot choice matters - worst case O(n squared) if pivot is always min or max. Average case is O(n log n).
errorTips:
Pivot: last element in this implementation
Partition: less and greater lists based on pivot
Recurse on both partitions, combine with pivot in middle
Quick sort is in-place (less memory) vs merge sort which needs extra arrays

quizQuestions.q: What is the worst case for quick sort and when does it happen?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: O(n squared) when the pivot chosen is always the smallest or largest element (e.g., already sorted list with bad pivot strategy). Average case is O(n log n). Good pivot strategies (random, median-of-three) prevent worst case.

quizQuestions.q: Quick sort is usually faster than merge sort in practice. Why?
options: B) It is in-place (no extra arrays needed) and has good cache locality
D) It always picks the best pivot

quizQuestions.q: What does partition do in quick sort?
options: B) Splits the list into two parts: elements smaller than pivot and elements larger than pivot

whyThisMatters: O(1) - constant (instant). O(log n) - binary search. O(n) - linear (each element once). O(n log n) - merge/quick sort. O(n squared) - nested loops (bubble sort). O(2 power n) - exponential. O(n factorial) - factorial. As n grows, higher complexity dominates.

O(2 power n) and O(n factorial) - only for very small n

# ========================================
# QUEST LOG ANALYZER
# Level 75 — File I/O & OS
# Concept: Reading and writing structured files — CSV and JSON
# ========================================
# Read quest logs stored as JSON and CSV. Parse structured game data files.
#
# Theory: CSV files: import csv; with open('data.csv') as f: reader = csv.DictReader(f); for row in reader: print(row['column']). JSON files: import json; with open('data.json') as f: data = json.load(f) - returns dict/list. Writing: csv.DictWriter, json.dump().
#
# Write your code below each task.
# Press Run to test your solution.
# ========================================

# import csv
with open('crew.csv', 'w', newline='') as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=['name', 'rank'])
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerow({'name': 'Alice', 'rank': 'Captain'})
    writer.writerow({'name': 'Bob', 'rank': 'Engineer'})

with open('crew.csv', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())

# import json
data = {'pilot': 'Alice', 'score': 950, 'missions': 12}
with open('pilot.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(data, f)

with open('pilot.json', 'r') as f:
    loaded = json.load(f)
print(loaded['pilot'], loaded['score'])

# import csv
with open('crew.csv', 'r') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(row['name'], row['rank'])
errorTips:
csv.DictReader reads rows as dictionaries - column names from header
csv.DictWriter needs fieldnames - writeheader() first
json.load() reads JSON into Python dict/list
json.dump() writes Python dict/list as JSON

quizQuestions.q: Why should you use newline='' when opening a CSV file for writing in Python?
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Without newline='', Python may add extra blank rows on Windows because the CSV writer uses \r\n line endings by default. The blank '' prevents this platform-specific issue. This is a known Python 3 behavior.

quizQuestions.q: What does json.load(f) return if the JSON file contains {"score": 100}?
